content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Every organisation accumulates knowledge over time. Some of it lives in documents, some in photographs, and some in recordings that capture moments, decisions and events that would otherwise be lost.
At CommBank, more than a century of history is preserved by the Group Archives team across a vast collection of records, including thousands of hours of footage stored on VHS and Betamax tapes. These recordings provide a unique window into Australia’s past, from historic advertising campaigns and major company milestones to everyday moments that reflect how banking and society have changed over time.
The challenge is that magnetic tape does not last forever. As physical media deteriorates and playback equipment becomes increasingly difficult to maintain, the opportunity to preserve and understand these recordings is rapidly disappearing. While digitisation protects the footage itself, making thousands of hours of video searchable and accessible remains a significant task.
Project ECHO was built to help solve that problem. Developed by our AI Labs team and NextGen AI Automation, it is a multi-modal video annotation pipeline that uses open-weight models running on a single 24GB GPU to transcribe, describe and catalogue more than 12,000 hours of digitised archive footage, helping transform a historical collection into a searchable resource for future generations.
Digitisation is the critical first step, but to unlock the full value of this archive, these videos must also be searchable and accessible. Through Project ECHO (Enhancing Cultural Heritage with Open Artificial Intelligence), these digitised materials become an indexable, institutional memory bank that helps future generations look back on CommBank’s role within Australia’s history and culture.
Building a multi-modal video annotation pipeline
The AI Labs & NextGen AI Automation teams partnered with the CommBank Archives team to build a multi-modal pipeline using free and open-weight models for transcription and scene description of digitised film. In a GenAI plot twist, this partnership arose after CommBank’s head archivist discovered the AI Labs team by talking to our internal Copilot.
As with many archives in the public and private sectors, cost efficiency was the key point of optimisation. Of course, this presented a challenge but also an attractive opportunity for the dev team to showcase the multi-modal capabilities of open-weight models at a time when tokenomics is coming sharply into focus. Here’s what we built:
System Architecture

• The Group Archives team extracts digitised media and associated OPEX metadata from Preservica, a proprietary archival database, and uploads it to an internal web portal which stores the media file and the metadata in AWS S3 storage and a DynamoDB database respectively.

• Every evening a batch job is kicked off as an ECS task, which:

• Retrieves unprocessed media files from the S3 storage

• Cuts out keyframes (approximate scene cut frames) from the video file using ffmpeg’s scene filter

• Resamples the audio stream to Whisper’s native 16kHz mono

• Calls the vLLM endpoints for the transcription and description
3. Finally, we save the output artefacts to DynamoDB and notify the Archives team by email.
To help the team review the scene descriptions easily we mapped the JSON output to a nice frontend rendered with markdown tables and headings.
Lastly, the descriptions and transcriptions are fed back into Preservica to enrich the existing records.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

System architecture diagram for the projectFor our compute, we were running an EC2 g5.xlarge instance equipped with 24GB of VRAM. To satisfy our memory constraints, we chose the Google Gemma 4 E2B model for image description and OpenAI’s Whisper Large v3 Turbo for speech-to-text transcription.
We opted for vLLM as our inference server stack as it handles concurrent requests cleanly with continuous batching and we could stream keyframes and audio directly to the OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
We retained both models in VRAM using separate vLLM processes concurrently and deferred to the GPU scheduler to optimise throughput. Worth noting, this GPU only costs $1.308 USD/hour to rent on demand.
We also considered generating text and image embeddings to build a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) search index. However, we prioritised plain-text outputs because they are optimal for subject matter experts to review, edit, and feed back into existing archival workflows.
Scene descriptions
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A screencap from the scene description testing tool in full early-CGI gloryOur output formats were intended to be both human and machine parseable. Because the output would be reviewed by archivists and later ingested by archival systems, we wanted a clean and predictable schema.
For the scene descriptions, we use vLLM’s Structured Outputs with a Pydantic schema to enforce a structured JSON output.
from typing import Annotated, Literal
from pydantic import BaseModel, Fieldclass KeyframeDescription(BaseModel):
"""Structured description of a single digitised keyframe."""
scene_description: Annotated[
str,
Field(description="Detailed visual description of what is happening in the frame.", max_length=800),
]
content_type: Annotated[
Literal[
"talking_head", "news_segment", "advertisement", "sports",
"music_performance", "title_card", "b_roll", "documentary",
"home_video", "other",
],
Field(description="Best-fit category for this frame's content."),
]
setting: Annotated[
Literal[
"Advertisement",
"Annual General Meeting",
"Branch",
"Forum",
"Indoor",
"Interview",
"Office",
"Outdoor",
"Panel Discussion",
"Presentation",
"Studio",
"Other",
],
Field(description="Physical environment or scene setting. Pick the single best match. Use 'Other' only if none of the specific options fit."),
]
people_count: Annotated[
int,
Field(ge=0, le=50, description="Number of visible people in the frame."),
]
text_visible: Annotated[
list[Annotated[str, Field(max_length=200)]],
Field(
description=(
"All legible text found in the frame, including captions, chyrons, title cards, "
"logos, lower-thirds, signs, etc. Empty list if none."
),
max_length=20,
),
]
colour_mode: Annotated[
Literal["colour", "black_and_white", "sepia"],
Field(description="Colour presentation of the frame."),
]
Note, that we can apply length limits on the fields to prevent unbounded generation and help us keep to our conservative token output budget.
To optimise our throughput, we pass the extracted keyframes to the vLLM Gemma endpoint using an in-memory BytesIO representation instead of writing the image files to disk first. Extracted keyframes are streamed directly to the vLLM endpoint in a batch size of 12. This ensures near constant 100% GPU utilisation for image processing. Finally, we optimally prefetch subsequent media files (these are between 500MB to 6GB file size) so that there is no waiting gap between processing consecutive files.
We also kept the stable part of the prompt at the front and appended dynamic video metadata at the end. This allowed vLLM to reuse the cached prefix across requests, reducing repeated prompt-processing prefill overhead.
Each frame took just over 1 second to process, end-to-end. Google has also recently released assistant Gemma models to increase token decoding speed by guess-and-checking with speculative decoding. Unfortunately, speculative decoding is not yet configured for non-text modalities, so we weren’t able to evaluate it.
We observed that Gemma 4 E2B occasionally misidentified certain historical objects, such as typewriters. The larger Gemma models did not exhibit the same issue, and our human-in-the-loop workflow allows the Archives team to correct any misidentifications before outputs are released.
Speech-to-Text (STT) transcription
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A screencap of the user-facing dashboard where archivists can check and correct transcriptsvLLM has a clean ‘audio tower’ interface built-in that handles chunking and audible-speech boundary detection. Thankfully, most of the audio was high-quality recordings, meaning we didn’t need to aggressively pre-process to account for noise.
However, since some of our videos were extremely long (exceeding 3 hours) we resampled the audio from 48kHz stereo to Whisper’s native 16kHz mono to decrease audio file sizes. The transcriptions were generated with timestamps, to allow us to output a clean SubRip (SRT) subtitle file for the Archives team to align the video to the audio.
We selected Whisper as our STT model because it has built-in vLLM support and a relatively low memory footprint. However, we are also evaluating newer STT models. One promising option is Microsoft’s VibeVoice, which supports “hotwords”: predefined terms that can improve transcription accuracy for domain-specific vocabulary. For example, Australian English accents are relatively underrepresented in many training corpora, and Whisper struggled to correctly transcribe the phrase “Crocodile Dundee”.
On a related note, due to their training corpus, the models default to transcribing text using American English spelling and grammar. Thankfully this is easy to fix at the prompt level.
In our testing, we managed to hit a faster-than-real-time factor of approximately 10x to 20x (excluding upstream media extraction and downstream review).
What’s next?
From requirements gathering to deployment, the whole project took about 6 weeks.
At AI Labs, we’re excited about the role that open-weight models will play in the preservation of historical materials. Smaller models have evolved from academic toys to being capable of producing quality output that’s consumable by both humans and AI frameworks alike.
We believe that fitting the model size to the problem unlocks performance at price.
This is also an example of using AI as a force multiplier. The annotation work would be impossible relying on humans alone, but this pipeline amplifies specialist archival expertise at scale.
“The ECHO pipeline will save us about 12,000 hours of skilled transcription and description work, which is well beyond our capacity as a small five-person team.”
Jeff Palmer, Group Archivist
We welcome interest from small archives, museums and not-for-profit organisations who are exploring new ways to process and unlock their digital AV collections. If this resonates, we’d be pleased to hear from you and explore how we can assist your work through our ECHO initiative. You can contact the team on archives@cba.com.au.
Authors
Lachy Berry — Data Scientist at CommBank.
Xun Wang — Principal Engineer at CommBank.
Yossi Frenkel — Data Scientist at CommBank.
